free_text: Emission of intermediate mass fragments during fission
#doi:10.1103/PhysRevC.54.R2114
He3 particle beam data.
The Indiana University Cyclotron
Facility.
The beam intensity was typically ~9x10**9 p/s.
A self-supporting ~700 microg/cm2 232Th foil.
Each IMF telescope consisted
of an axial-field ionization chamber operated at 18
torr of CF4, a 300 microm passivated ion-implanted
silicon detector and a 3 cm thick CsI(Tl) crystal
readout with a photodiode. The small Si detectors
each had an active area of 3 cm x 3 cm. The large Si
detectors each had an active area of 5 cm x 5 cm and
consisted of a quadrant design.
Charged particles that entered the silicon
detector were identified by the dE-E technique. In
addition, charged particles were also mass-identified
by utilizing the time-of-flight technique with
reference to the cyclotron RF pulse. The timing
resolution achieved in the experiment was sufficient
to resolve isotopes of elements with Z<4. Energy
calibration of the detectors was performed by use of
a 228Th a source and a precision pulser.

Fig. 3, page R2115.
Low-energy/focused
emission.
The cross-section was calculated from the number of
interactions, the integrated beam current, and the
target thickness and agreed with the previously
measured binary fission cross section to within 5%.
The efficiency for detecting ternary events was
calculated using the measured IMF energy
distribution. The angular distribution of neck
fragments was assumed to be a Gaussian with a width
of 15 degrees.
We estimate the uncertainty in cross
section to be less than 10%.
Fig. 5a, page R2117.
High-energy/isotropic
emission.
The cross-section was calculated from the number of
interactions, the integrated beam current, and the
target thickness and agreed with the previously
measured binary fission cross section to within 5%.
The efficiency for detecting ternary events was
calculated using the measured IMF energy
distribution. The angular distribution of neck
fragments was assumed to be a Gaussian with a width
of 15 degrees.
We estimate the uncertainty in cross
section to be less than 10%.
